title: Software Requirements
text:
The use of Colab Notebooks for teaching makes software requirements essentially non-existent. However, some students may want to install the Python ecosystem locally on their own machines for various reasons:

Script-based workflow: You may prefer to code from scripts directly, rather than using the cell-based philosophy of Colab notebooks. Managing complex code and data pipelines can become cumbersome in a notebook environment.
Computational limitations: The Colab environment is free, but that comes at a cost: the virtual machine running your code is very limited in resources (CPU, RAM, and disk space). For heavy computing or very complex tasks, it may be impractical or impossible to use.
Package compatibility: You may need to install packages that are incompatible with the Colab virtual machine environment or that are very difficult to set up within it.
Data storage needs: You may require live access to data repositories and need to read from or write large amounts of data, which can be constrained in the cloud-based Colab environment.

For the above reasons, you may need to install and run Python locally. Two common ways to do so are:
1. Install Python directly from python.org
This is simple and provides you with a basic environment and an IDE (IDLE). (IDE: Integrated development environment - a software application, similar to Rstudio, that offers and integrated and self-contained approach to software development).
2. Install Conda or Miniconda from anaconda.com
This gives you a much more powerful environment with access to virtually thousands of packages, along with the benefit of isolated environments—greatly reducing the risk of conflicts with other software installed on your machine. Tricky packages are often much easier to install in Conda than in a bare Python environment.

Additional Notes:

Linux or Mac users: You already have Python installed. You only need to install additional packages (such as NumPy, pandas, Matplotlib, Seaborn, or SciPy) beyond what is installed by default on these systems. It is recommended to install these packages in virtual environments to isolate different software requirements and distributions.
Notebook-based workflow: If you prefer a local installation but still want to work in a notebook-based environment similar to Colab (a common preference among data scientists), you can install Jupyter under your Python installation. This will allow you to create, save, and run Python notebooks locally. (Colab notebooks are, in fact, Jupyter notebooks with some Google-specific tweaks.)

Should you decide to pursue a local installation, the teacher can provide assistance.

title: Python Programming
text:
General Information
Welcome to the home of the Python Programming Module of the Computational Biology Course. In this module, we will learn the basics of Python programming and provide an introduction to Python for data analytics and statistical analysis using a variety of specialized Python packages. The module does not require any previous programming knowledge, although this can certainly be helpful. Prior knowledge of basic concepts in statistics (descriptive statistics, distributions, simple hypothesis testing) is required and has been provided in the Statistics module of this course. The Python and R programming languages share many common concepts and methods, and students can certainly leverage their newly acquired R skills in their Python learning. To facilitate this transition, an R-to-Python comparative table is available.



Teaching Approach
This is a hands-on course, centered on student activities with guidance from the teacher. The teaching sessions use Colab Notebooks, a coding environment created by Google that requires minimal setup on the students’ part. The course consists of a series of Colab Notebooks that students will work through, replicating calculations and solving simple coding challenges. Refer to the Notebooks Page for full instructions on how to use them.



Module Evaluation
Student performance in this module will be evaluated by means of an exam administered through the online tutorial system at the University of Algarve (which is Moodle-based). This exam will simultaneously evaluate both the Statistics and the Python Programming modules. There will be multiple-choice questions as well as questions in which students will be asked to write a small piece of code to perform a particular task (students will be allowed to consult a reference table with Python commands).
PhD students enrolling in this course will be subject to an additional evaluation step. After finishing the exam, they will be given a simple data analysis exercise and will be requested to produce a Colab Notebook containing the analysis and explanatory comments. The Notebooks thus created will be shared with the teacher for evaluation.

title: Notebooks
text:
This page lists the Google Colab Notebooks used in this course. To use them, students must have an active Google account. By clicking on one of the links below, students will open a copy of the notebook in their browser (Chrome is recommended, but it should work in any browser). Since they are viewing a notebook created by another person (the teacher, in this case), they need to use the option File > Save a Copy in Drive to create a copy of the document in their own Google Drive. After that, any edits made to the notebook will be preserved.
Once copied, the notebook needs to be activated—that is, it must be connected to a virtual machine running on the Google Cloud. In this way, students are provided with a full computing environment without the need to install any software on their computers. Keep in mind, however, that any files created on the virtual machine will be erased once the machine disconnects. Therefore, anything that needs to be kept must be either downloaded or saved to Google Drive.
To activate the notebook, click the Connect button in the upper-right corner.

List of Colab Notebooks:

Introduction to Python Programming (for the absolute beginner)
Introduction to Numpy
Introduction to Pandas
Descriptive Statistics
